hook.text: In 1989, a physicist at CERN wanted to share research papers. He invented the World Wide Web instead.

buildup.text: Tim Berners-Lee combined hypertext, TCP/IP, and DNS into one system: a web of linked documents.

discovery.text: He created HTML for pages, HTTP for transfer, and URLs for addresses. Three pillars of the web.

twist.text: CERN made the web free for everyone in 1993. No patents, no royalties. A gift to humanity.

climax.text: The first website described the web project itself. It still runs at info.cern.ch — the web's origin.

punchline.text: One physicist's idea to share papers became the modern web.

quiz.question: What three technologies did Tim Berners-Lee create for the web?
options: HTML, HTTP, and URLs
TCP, IP, and DNS
JavaScript, CSS, and PHP

hook.text: Every page on the web has an address. URLs are the coordinates that take you to any resource online.

buildup.text: A URL has parts: scheme (https), host (example.com), path (/page), and optional query (?key=value).

discovery.text: The browser resolves the host to an IP via DNS, then uses the path to request a specific resource.

twist.text: Percent encoding handles special characters. A space becomes %20. That's why URLs look ugly sometimes.

climax.text: Fragment identifiers (#section) navigate within a page. They never leave the browser — the server never sees them.

punchline.text: URLs address every resource on the web. Simple, universal, powerful.

quiz.question: What part of a URL does the server never see?
options: The fragment identifier after the # symbol
The scheme (https)
The domain name

hook.text: Every web page is an HTML document. Tags define headings, paragraphs, links, images, and structure.

buildup.text: HTML uses nested tags: <html> wraps <head> and <body>. The browser reads this tree and renders a page.

discovery.text: Semantic tags like <article>, <nav>, and <footer> give meaning. Screen readers and search engines rely on them.

twist.text: HTML alone is ugly. It provides structure, not style. CSS adds the colors, layout, and typography.

climax.text: HTML5 added video, audio, and canvas natively. Plugins like Flash became obsolete overnight.

punchline.text: HTML is the skeleton of every web page. Structure first, style later.

quiz.question: What did HTML5 make obsolete for playing video on the web?
options: Browser plugins like Flash
CSS stylesheets
JavaScript frameworks

hook.text: When you click a link, your browser sends an HTTP request. The server sends back an HTTP response. That's the web.

buildup.text: HTTP methods define actions: GET fetches data, POST submits data, PUT updates, DELETE removes.

discovery.text: Status codes tell the outcome: 200 OK, 301 Moved, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.

twist.text: HTTP is stateless. The server forgets you after each request. Cookies are the workaround for sessions.

climax.text: Headers carry metadata: content type, caching rules, auth tokens. They're invisible but control everything.

punchline.text: HTTP is request and response. The simplest protocol that runs the web.

quiz.question: Why is HTTP called stateless?
options: The server does not remember previous requests
It cannot transfer files larger than 1 MB
It only works over wireless connections

hook.text: HTML builds the skeleton. CSS paints it. Every color, font, and layout on the web comes from CSS.

buildup.text: Selectors target HTML elements. Properties set values: color, margin, font-size, display, and hundreds more.

discovery.text: The cascade resolves conflicts: inline beats internal, internal beats external. Specificity is the tiebreaker.

twist.text: Responsive design uses media queries. One stylesheet adapts the layout to phones, tablets, and desktops.

climax.text: Flexbox and Grid transformed layout. Before them, developers hacked layouts with floats and tables.

punchline.text: CSS makes the web beautiful. Separation of structure and style.

quiz.question: What CSS feature enables layouts that adapt to different screen sizes?
options: Media queries
Inline styles
HTML5 semantic tags

hook.text: Type a URL, press Enter, and a page appears. Behind that moment are dozens of steps in milliseconds.

buildup.text: The browser resolves DNS, opens a TCP connection, performs a TLS handshake, then sends the HTTP request.

discovery.text: The server responds with HTML. The browser parses it into a DOM tree, then fetches CSS, JS, and images.

twist.text: CSS blocks rendering until it's parsed. JS blocks parsing unless marked async or defer. Order matters hugely.

climax.text: The render tree combines DOM and CSSOM. The browser calculates layout, paints pixels, and composites layers.

punchline.text: URL to pixels in under a second. A symphony of protocols.

quiz.question: What blocks HTML parsing in the browser by default?
options: JavaScript that is not marked async or defer
Image files referenced in the HTML
CSS custom properties

hook.text: JavaScript makes the web interactive. The engine is the brain that runs it — fast, compiled, and optimized.

buildup.text: V8 (Chrome), SpiderMonkey (Firefox), and JavaScriptCore (Safari) compile JS to machine code at runtime.

discovery.text: JIT compilation profiles hot code paths and optimizes them. Cold paths run interpreted for quick startup.

twist.text: Garbage collection reclaims unused memory. Pauses used to freeze pages. Modern GCs work incrementally.

climax.text: V8 powers Node.js too. The same engine that runs your browser also runs server-side JavaScript.

punchline.text: JS engines JIT-compile code at runtime. Fast enough for apps.

quiz.question: What technique do modern JS engines use to maximize performance?
options: JIT compilation that optimizes hot code paths
Pre-compiling all JavaScript before the page loads
Running JavaScript in a separate thread by default

hook.text: HTTP forgets you instantly. Cookies are small files that make the stateless web remember who you are.

buildup.text: A server sends a Set-Cookie header. The browser stores the cookie and sends it with every future request.

discovery.text: Session cookies expire when you close the browser. Persistent cookies survive for days, months, or years.

twist.text: Third-party cookies track you across sites. Ad networks use them to build profiles. Privacy disaster.

climax.text: Chrome is phasing out third-party cookies. Privacy Sandbox aims to replace them with less invasive APIs.

punchline.text: Cookies add memory to a forgetful protocol. Useful, but privacy-risky.

quiz.question: Why are third-party cookies a privacy concern?
options: They let ad networks track users across multiple websites
They slow down page load times significantly
They can execute malicious code on your computer

hook.text: Visit a site again and it loads faster. Your browser cached the files the first time. No re-download needed.

buildup.text: Cache-Control headers tell the browser how long to keep a file. max-age=3600 means cache for one hour.

discovery.text: ETags let the browser ask: has this file changed? The server responds 304 Not Modified if it hasn't.

twist.text: Aggressive caching causes stale content. Cache-busting adds a hash to filenames so updates are forced.

climax.text: Service workers give you full cache control in JavaScript. You can serve pages even when offline.

punchline.text: Caching speeds up the web. The trick is knowing when to let go.

quiz.question: What HTTP status code means the cached version is still valid?
options: 304 Not Modified
200 OK
404 Not Found

hook.text: Press F12 and a hidden world opens. DevTools let you inspect, debug, and profile any web page live.

buildup.text: The Elements panel shows live HTML and CSS. Edit styles in real time and see changes instantly.

discovery.text: The Network panel shows every HTTP request: timing, size, status code, and headers. Debug slow loads here.

twist.text: The Console runs JavaScript on the live page. Read variables, call functions, test snippets without reloading.

climax.text: Lighthouse audits performance, accessibility, and SEO. It scores your page and gives fix suggestions.

punchline.text: DevTools turn any browser into a web development lab. Free and built in.

quiz.question: What does the Network panel in DevTools show?
options: All HTTP requests with their timing and status codes
The page's HTML source code only
A list of installed browser extensions

hook.text: Web apps don't reload pages to fetch data. They call REST APIs and update the screen dynamically.

buildup.text: REST uses standard HTTP methods: GET to read, POST to create, PUT to update, DELETE to remove resources.

discovery.text: Resources are identified by URLs. GET /users/42 returns user 42. POST /users creates a new one.

twist.text: REST is stateless. Each request carries all needed info. The server stores no session between calls.

climax.text: JSON is the standard response format. Lightweight, readable, and native to JavaScript. XML lost this battle.

punchline.text: REST maps CRUD to HTTP. Simple, scalable, universal.

quiz.question: What HTTP method does REST use to create a new resource?
options: POST
GET
DELETE

hook.text: REST gives you fixed endpoints. GraphQL lets you ask for exactly the data you need. Nothing more.

buildup.text: A GraphQL query specifies fields. The server returns only those fields. No over-fetching, no under-fetching.

discovery.text: One endpoint handles everything. Instead of 5 REST calls for a page, send 1 GraphQL query.

twist.text: The server needs a schema defining every type and field. More setup than REST. Power has a cost.

climax.text: Facebook created GraphQL in 2012 for mobile apps where bandwidth matters. It's now used by GitHub and Shopify.

punchline.text: GraphQL: ask for what you need, get exactly that. Precise and efficient.

quiz.question: What problem does GraphQL solve that REST does not?
options: Over-fetching and under-fetching of data
Server-side rendering of HTML pages
Encrypting API requests automatically

hook.text: HTTP is request-response. But chat apps need the server to push messages instantly. WebSockets do that.

buildup.text: A WebSocket starts as an HTTP request, then upgrades to a persistent two-way connection.

discovery.text: Once open, both sides send messages freely. No request needed. Data flows in real time, low latency.

twist.text: WebSockets stay open for hours. HTTP connections close after each response. Different lifetime, different use.

climax.text: Stock tickers, multiplayer games, and collaborative editors all rely on WebSocket connections.

punchline.text: WebSockets keep the line open. Real-time web, no polling.

quiz.question: How does a WebSocket connection begin?
options: As an HTTP request that upgrades to a persistent connection
As a direct TCP socket bypassing HTTP entirely
As a UDP packet sent to the server

hook.text: One giant app does everything. It breaks, and everything goes down. Microservices split the work up.

buildup.text: Each microservice handles one task: auth, payments, search. They communicate via APIs over HTTP or gRPC.

discovery.text: Teams deploy services independently. Update search without touching payments. Faster releases.

twist.text: Microservices add complexity: service discovery, distributed tracing, and network failures between services.

climax.text: Netflix runs 700+ microservices. Amazon has thousands. Scale demands decomposition.

punchline.text: Microservices trade monolith simplicity for scale and speed.

quiz.question: What is a key advantage of microservices over a monolith?
options: Services can be deployed and updated independently
They require less network communication
They eliminate the need for APIs

hook.text: Your server is in Virginia. A user in Tokyo waits 200ms per request. A CDN serves from Tokyo instead.

buildup.text: CDNs cache content on edge servers worldwide. Users connect to the nearest one for faster delivery.

discovery.text: Static assets — images, CSS, JS — are cached globally. The origin server is hit only on cache misses.

twist.text: Modern CDNs run code at the edge: Cloudflare Workers and Vercel Edge Functions compute near the user.

climax.text: CDNs also absorb DDoS attacks. Distributed infrastructure can handle traffic spikes your origin can't.

punchline.text: CDNs put your content everywhere. Closer to the user means faster.

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of a CDN?
options: Serving content from servers closer to the user
Encrypting all data on the origin server
Replacing the origin server entirely

hook.text: Google measures your site's speed with three metrics. They affect your ranking. Meet the Core Web Vitals.

buildup.text: LCP — Largest Contentful Paint. How fast the main content appears. Target: under 2.5 seconds.

discovery.text: INP — Interaction to Next Paint. How fast the page responds to clicks. Target: under 200 milliseconds.

twist.text: CLS — Cumulative Layout Shift. How much the page jumps around while loading. Target: under 0.1.

climax.text: Chrome collects real user data (CrUX). Google uses it for ranking. Lab scores help debug, field data decides rank.

punchline.text: Core Web Vitals: how fast, how responsive, how stable. Three scores that matter.

quiz.question: What does LCP measure?
options: How fast the largest visible content element loads
How many layout shifts occur during page load
How quickly the page responds to user input

hook.text: A page has 50 images but only 3 are visible. Why load all 50? Lazy loading defers the rest until needed.

buildup.text: The loading='lazy' attribute on tells the browser to load images only when they enter the viewport.

discovery.text: Intersection Observer API gives precise control. Trigger loads when elements are N pixels from view.

twist.text: Lazy load too aggressively and above-the-fold images appear blank. Exempt hero images from lazy loading.

climax.text: Code splitting lazy-loads JavaScript too. Import components only when the user navigates to them.

punchline.text: Load what's visible. Defer the rest. Faster for everyone.

quiz.question: What HTML attribute enables native lazy loading for images?
options: loading='lazy'
defer='true'
async='image'

hook.text: Images make up 50%+ of most page weight. Optimize them and your site gets dramatically faster.

buildup.text: Modern formats like WebP and AVIF compress 30-50% smaller than JPEG at the same quality. Use them.

discovery.text: Responsive images with srcset serve small files to phones and large files to desktops. Right size, right device.

twist.text: Width and height attributes prevent layout shift. The browser reserves space before the image downloads.

climax.text: CDNs like Cloudinary and imgix optimize images on the fly: resize, compress, and convert per request.

punchline.text: Smaller images, faster pages. Optimize format, size, and delivery.

quiz.question: What advantage does WebP have over JPEG?
options: 30-50% smaller file size at similar quality
It supports animation while JPEG does not
It loads without an HTTP request

hook.text: HTTP/1.1 loads one thing at a time per connection. HTTP/2 loads many in parallel on one connection.

buildup.text: HTTP/2 uses multiplexing: multiple streams share one TCP connection. No more head-of-line blocking at HTTP level.

discovery.text: Header compression with HPACK shrinks repetitive headers. Server push preloads resources before the browser asks.

twist.text: TCP still has head-of-line blocking. One lost packet stalls all streams. HTTP/3 fixes this with QUIC over UDP.

climax.text: HTTP/3 uses QUIC: encrypted by default, faster handshakes, and independent streams. Packet loss affects only one.

punchline.text: HTTP/2 multiplexes. HTTP/3 eliminates TCP's limits. Faster web, less waiting.

quiz.question: What transport protocol does HTTP/3 use instead of TCP?
options: QUIC over UDP
TCP with TLS 1.3
SCTP over IP

hook.text: What if your web app worked offline? Service workers intercept network requests and serve cached responses.

buildup.text: A service worker is JavaScript that runs in the background, separate from the page. It acts as a proxy.

discovery.text: On install, it pre-caches critical files. On fetch, it serves from cache first, then updates from the network.

twist.text: Service workers power push notifications and background sync too. They make web apps feel native.

climax.text: PWAs — Progressive Web Apps — use service workers to install on your home screen and work offline.

punchline.text: Service workers make the web work offline. Apps without the app store.

quiz.question: What role does a service worker play in a web app?
options: It intercepts network requests and can serve cached responses
It compiles JavaScript into native code
It manages the browser's tab layout

hook.text: The 'S' in HTTPS means your connection is encrypted. Without it, anyone on the network can read your data.

buildup.text: TLS wraps HTTP in encryption. The server proves its identity with a certificate signed by a trusted authority.

discovery.text: TLS 1.3 completes the handshake in one round trip. Faster than TLS 1.2, which needed two round trips.

twist.text: Let's Encrypt made certificates free and automated. HTTPS went from expensive to default in five years.

climax.text: Browsers now mark HTTP sites as 'Not Secure.' Over 95% of Chrome page loads use HTTPS today.

punchline.text: HTTPS encrypts the web. Free certificates made it universal.

quiz.question: How did Let's Encrypt change HTTPS adoption?
options: It made TLS certificates free and automated
It created a faster version of the TLS protocol
It blocked all HTTP traffic on the internet

hook.text: An attacker injects JavaScript into a trusted website. When you visit, the script runs in your browser.

buildup.text: Stored XSS saves malicious code in a database. Every user who views the page gets attacked.

discovery.text: Reflected XSS hides the script in a URL. Click the link and the server echoes the attack back to you.

twist.text: XSS steals cookies, session tokens, and personal data. It's the most common web vulnerability for years.

climax.text: Content Security Policy headers restrict which scripts can run. Combined with output encoding, XSS is preventable.

punchline.text: XSS injects scripts into trusted sites. Sanitize all user input.

quiz.question: What does a Content Security Policy header help prevent?
options: Unauthorized scripts from running on the page
SQL queries from reaching the database
Users from accessing pages without logging in

hook.text: Your JavaScript tries to call another domain's API. The browser blocks it. CORS is why — and the fix.

buildup.text: Same-origin policy blocks cross-origin requests by default. It prevents a malicious page from stealing your data.

discovery.text: CORS lets servers opt in. The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header tells browsers which origins are allowed.

twist.text: Preflight OPTIONS requests check permission before the real request. The browser sends them automatically.

climax.text: Misconfigured CORS — like allowing all origins — undoes the entire security model. Be specific.

punchline.text: CORS controls cross-origin access. The server decides who's allowed.

quiz.question: What HTTP header does a server use to allow cross-origin requests?
options: Access-Control-Allow-Origin
Content-Security-Policy
X-Frame-Options

hook.text: 'Sign in with Google' is everywhere. OAuth lets you log in to apps without sharing your password.

buildup.text: OAuth redirects you to the identity provider. You log in there. It sends a token back to the app.

discovery.text: The app gets an access token, not your password. The token grants limited permissions for a limited time.

twist.text: OAuth is for authorization, not authentication. OpenID Connect adds identity on top. They're often confused.

climax.text: JWTs carry claims in a signed token. The server verifies the signature without calling the identity provider.

punchline.text: OAuth delegates login. Your password stays with the provider you trust.

quiz.question: What does an app receive from OAuth instead of the user's password?
options: A time-limited access token with specific permissions
A copy of the user's password hash
Full access to the user's identity provider account

hook.text: Type a name into a form. But instead of a name, type SQL code. If the server runs it, you own the database.

buildup.text: SQL injection happens when user input is inserted into a query without sanitization. The input becomes code.

discovery.text: A classic attack: ' OR 1=1 -- . This makes the WHERE clause always true, returning all rows.

twist.text: Parameterized queries separate code from data. The input is always treated as a value, never as SQL.

climax.text: SQL injection remains in OWASP's Top 10 after 20 years. Old, well-known, and still exploited.

punchline.text: Never trust user input. Parameterize every query. Always.

quiz.question: What is the best defense against SQL injection?
options: Using parameterized queries that separate data from code
Limiting the length of user input fields
Encrypting the database connection with TLS

hook.text: JavaScript is fast. But for Photoshop in a browser, you need faster. WebAssembly runs at near-native speed.

buildup.text: Wasm is a binary format. Browsers execute it alongside JavaScript. C, C++, and Rust compile to Wasm.

discovery.text: Figma, Google Earth, and AutoCAD run in the browser via Wasm. Desktop-class apps, no install needed.

twist.text: Wasm doesn't replace JavaScript. They work together. JS handles the UI. Wasm handles heavy computation.

climax.text: WASI extends Wasm beyond browsers. Run Wasm on servers, edge, and IoT. A universal runtime is emerging.

punchline.text: Wasm brings native performance to the web. The browser is the new OS.

quiz.question: What languages can compile to WebAssembly?
options: C, C++, and Rust among others
Only JavaScript
Only Python and Ruby

hook.text: Why send a request to a data center 5,000 miles away? Edge computing runs code close to the user.

buildup.text: Edge nodes sit in ISP facilities and CDN PoPs. They run serverless functions milliseconds from the user.

discovery.text: Cloudflare Workers run in 300+ cities. Vercel Edge Functions deploy globally. Latency drops dramatically.

twist.text: Edge has limits: small memory, short execution times, and no persistent storage. It's not a full server.

climax.text: Edge handles auth, redirects, A/B tests, and personalization. Light logic, big speed gains.

punchline.text: Edge computing: less distance, less latency, faster web.

quiz.question: What is a limitation of edge computing?
options: Small memory, short execution times, and no persistent storage
It only works in the United States
It cannot serve HTTP responses

hook.text: Web1 was read-only. Web2 let users create content. Web3 promises user ownership via blockchain.

buildup.text: Web3 uses smart contracts on Ethereum and other chains. Apps run on decentralized networks, not company servers.

discovery.text: Users own their data and digital assets as tokens. No central authority controls access or content.

twist.text: In practice, Web3 relies on centralized services: Infura for nodes, OpenSea for NFTs, MetaMask for wallets.

climax.text: Scalability and UX remain major hurdles. Gas fees and seed phrases confuse mainstream users.

punchline.text: Web3 envisions a user-owned internet. The reality is still evolving.

quiz.question: What technology underlies Web3 applications?
options: Blockchain and smart contracts
Traditional SQL databases
Peer-to-peer file sharing only

topics.title: AI and the Future of the Web

hook.text: AI chatbots answer questions without sending you to a web page. Is the traditional web in danger?

buildup.text: Search engines now show AI-generated answers. Fewer clicks to websites. Traffic to publishers is dropping.

discovery.text: AI copilots generate code, write content, and design UIs. Entire web pages can be created from a prompt.

twist.text: The web feeds AI training data. Without websites, AI models have nothing to learn from. Symbiosis, not replacement.

climax.text: Structured data and APIs will matter more. AI needs machine-readable content, not just pretty HTML pages.

punchline.text: AI reshapes how we use the web, but the web still feeds AI.

quiz.question: How is AI changing web search behavior?
options: AI answers reduce the need to click through to websites
AI makes web pages load faster
AI translates all web pages to one language

hook.text: One billion people have disabilities. If your site isn't accessible, you've locked out 15% of humanity.

buildup.text: WCAG defines accessibility standards. Perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust — four principles.

discovery.text: Alt text describes images for screen readers. ARIA labels add context to interactive elements.

twist.text: Accessibility helps everyone. Captions help in noisy rooms. Keyboard navigation helps power users too.

climax.text: Legal mandates like ADA and EAA require accessible websites. Lawsuits against inaccessible sites are rising.

punchline.text: Accessible web is better web. Design for all, benefit everyone.

quiz.question: What does WCAG stand for?
options: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines
Web Communication Access Group
Wireless Content Availability Guide
